title: Idle Clicker Prestige Loops — Why Resetting Everything Feels Like Winning
angle: The psychology and game design behind prestige mechanics in idle/clicker games — why voluntary reset is the most compelling progression system ever designed
notes: Focus on the paradox: players voluntarily destroy progress and feel rewarded. Cover the dopamine loop of exponential growth hitting a wall, the prestige reset as a 'new game plus' with meta-currency, and how games like Cookie Clicker and Adventure Capitalist use layered prestige to create infinite retention. Touch on sunk cost inversion — the moment when loss becomes investment.

sources.title: The Math Behind Idle Games
sources.summary: Kongregate engineering breakdown of exponential growth curves, diminishing returns, and how prestige multipliers create a second-order growth system that resets the dopamine curve without losing the meta-progression feeling.

sources.title: Idle Games: The Mechanics and Monetization of Self-Playing Games
sources.summary: GDC talk covering core loop design in idle/clicker games. Key insight: prestige is not a reset — it is a promotion. Players trade linear progress for exponential potential, which is psychologically identical to a career advancement.

sources.title: r/incremental_games — Why prestige systems work
sources.summary: Community discussion on the emotional arc of prestige: frustration at the wall, temptation of the reset button, anxiety during the reset, euphoria at the accelerated restart. Multiple players describe prestige as 'the moment the game actually begins.'

sources.title: Cookie Clicker Wiki — Ascension Mechanics
sources.summary: Detailed breakdown of Cookie Clicker's heavenly chips system and ascension upgrade tree. Documents how each prestige layer adds a new decision space, turning a simple clicker into a strategic resource allocation game.

sources.title: Orteil on Designing Cookie Clicker's Ascension
sources.summary: Developer interview where Orteil describes the ascension system as a solution to the 'big number fatigue' problem — players stop feeling progress when numbers get too large, so resetting with a multiplier makes small numbers exciting again.

sources.title: The Psychology of Sunk Costs in Game Design
sources.summary: Academic paper examining how prestige mechanics invert the sunk cost fallacy: instead of players feeling trapped by investment, the prestige reset reframes all prior progress as an investment in future power, converting loss aversion into anticipation.
